coredata.dc:title: Plasma-processed CoSn/RGO nanocomposite: A low-cost and sustainable counter electrode for dye-sensitized solar cells
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 International Solar Energy Society. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: International Solar Energy Society. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Omelianovych, Oleksii
Larina, Liudmila L.
Oh, Hyo-Jun
Park, Eunhee
Dao, Van-Duong
Choi, Ho-Suk
coredata.dc:description: The high cost of state-of-the-art Pt counter electrodes (CEs) hinders the large-scale applications of dye-sensitized solar cells (DSCs). The development of Pt-free catalysts while maintaining state-of-the-art catalytic activity for CE materials is one mean to reduce costs. Here, CoxSn1-x/reduced graphene oxide (RGO) (0 ≤ x ≤ 1) nanohybrids were synthesized and employed as inexpensive, stable, and earth-abundant CEs in DSCs. The synthesis was performed through the plasma-assisted reduction of the oxygen functional groups of the graphene oxide along with the immobilization of bimetallic nanoparticles (NPs) on the surface of RGO. The optimization of the composition of the alloy NPs for the highest efficiency of DSC yields the Co0.9Sn0.1/RGO nanocomposite. The highest device performance correlates well with the experimentally obtained lowest charge transfer resistance in conjunction with the highest electrocatalytic activity of the Co0.9Sn0.1/RGO CE. The DSC employed the synthesized CE showed good stability over long term operation. Both the developed CoSn/RGO nanohybrids and the strategy used for their synthesis are cost-effective. Our results provide economically implementable and green nanotechnology for efficient and stable DSCs required for commercialization.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Plasma reduction
Bimatalic alloy CoxSn1-x
Reduced graphene oxide
Nanohybrids
Counter electrode
Dye-sensitized solar cells

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the CoxSn1-x (0 ≤ x ≤ 1) alloys hybridized on the RGO
Fabrication of the working electrodes and assembly of the DSCs
Photocurrent–voltage characteristics of DSCs: Efficiency trend with the CoxSn1-x/RGO CE
Electrochemical characterization of the CoxSn1-x/RGO CEs
Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and Tafel measurements
Morphology and structure of the CoSn/RGO CEs

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2020 International Solar Energy Society. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The scheme showing the route for the synthesis of the CoxSn1-x/RGO nanohybrids.
The J-V characteristics of the DSCs employing CoxSn1-x/RGO nanohybrids (0 ≤ x ≤ 1) CEs.

(a) CV curves with different counter electrodes at a scan rate of 20 mVs−1. Electrocatalytic characteristics shown from (b) Nyquist plots; inset: a magnified image of the area highlighted with a dot-line, (c) Bode-phase plots, and (d) Tafel curves of the CEs prepared with CoxSn1-x/RGO (0 ≤ x ≤ 1) and assembled into symmetrical dummy cells. Note: The legend for b-d is provided in (a).
CV analysis of the stability of the Co0.9Sn0.1/RGO CE by 100 cycles.
HR-SEM images of the CoxSn1-x/RGO nanohybrids. The atomic ratio of the metals in the alloy is denoted in the top corner of each SEM-image.

(a-c) HR-TEM images of Co0.9Sn0.1 NPs with different magnification. (d) FFT-image of the Co0.9Sn0.1 NP with labeled planes from where lattice constants were obtained. (e-f) HR-TEM images of the CoSn particle agglomerates with marked dimensions.
Photovoltaic parameters of DSCs employing different CEs. Characteristic parameters obtained from the electrochemical characterization of the set of CEs.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: ΔE: peak-to-peak separation potential, Rct : charge-transfer resistance, Zw : diffusion impedance, Rtrns : electron transport resistance in the graphene layer, Jsc : short-circuit current density, Voc : open-circuit voltage, PCE: power conversion efficiency.

head.ce:title: Plasma-processed CoSn/RGO nanocomposite: A low-cost and sustainable counter electrode for dye-sensitized solar cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering and Applied Chemistry, Chungnam National University, Daejeon 34134, South Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical Engineering and Applied Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering and Applied Chemistry, Chungnam National University, Daejeon 34134, South Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Biotechnology, Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Phenikaa University, Hanoi 10000, Viet Nam

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Biotechnology, Chemistry and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Biotechnology, Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Phenikaa University, Hanoi 10000, Viet Nam

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Phenikaa Research and Technology Institute (PRATI), A&A Green Phoenix Group, 167 Hoang Ngan, Hanoi 10000, Viet Nam
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Phenikaa Research and Technology Institute (PRATI), A&A Green Phoenix Group, 167 Hoang Ngan, Hanoi 10000, Viet Nam

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: CoxSn1-x/RGO nanohybrids synthesized using the plasma-assisted reduction are employed as counter electrodes in dye-sensitized solar cells. The optimization of the synthesis conditions has led to favorable morphology, which in turn resulted in higher catalytic activity.

The high cost of state-of-the-art Pt counter electrodes (CEs) hinders the large-scale applications of dye-sensitized solar cells (DSCs). The development of Pt-free catalysts while maintaining state-of-the-art catalytic activity for CE materials is one mean to reduce costs. Here, CoxSn1-x/reduced graphene oxide (RGO) (0 ≤ x ≤ 1) nanohybrids were synthesized and employed as inexpensive, stable, and earth-abundant CEs in DSCs. The synthesis was performed through the plasma-assisted reduction of the oxygen functional groups of the graphene oxide along with the immobilization of bimetallic nanoparticles (NPs) on the surface of RGO. The optimization of the composition of the alloy NPs for the highest efficiency of DSC yields the Co0.9Sn0.1/RGO nanocomposite. The highest device performance correlates well with the experimentally obtained lowest charge transfer resistance in conjunction with the highest electrocatalytic activity of the Co0.9Sn0.1/RGO CE. The DSC employed the synthesized CE showed good stability over long term operation. Both the developed CoSn/RGO nanohybrids and the strategy used for their synthesis are cost-effective. Our results provide economically implementable and green nanotechnology for efficient and stable DSCs required for commercialization.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, synthesis and application of carbon-based materials and their composites are attracting attention in the field of materials research. Various syntheses (Khojasteh et al., 2017; Tavakoli et al., 2015; Tavakoli and Salavati-Niasari, 2014) and comparative analyses (Mohandes and Salavati-Niasari, 2014a,b) are being introduced to fully understand possible configurations and their applications. Graphene, carbon nanotubes, and their composites are applied to many fields including photocatalysis (Mahdiani et al., 2017; Soofivand and Salavati-Niasari, 2017, 2015), bioactivity (Mohandes and Salavati-Niasari, 2014a,b; Safardoust-Hojaghan et al., 2017), and pollutant-degradation (Safajou et al., 2017; Safardoust-Hojaghan and Salavati-Niasari, 2017). Incorporation of the carbon may lead to the improvement of the desired properties of the product, such as free-carrier concentration (Teymourinia et al., 2017), optical properties (Mahdiani et al., 2018), and capacitance (Masjedi-Arani and Salavati-Niasari, 2017). As of DSC applications, Pt-free alloy/RGO nanocomposites are alternatives to the state-of-the-art CEs. The widely used strategy for altering the reactivity of metal catalysts is alloying with various transition metals (Ponec, 1983). This leads to the alteration of the electronic structure of the native metallic surface and enhancing the bonding geometries of the adsorbents (Rodriguez and Goodman, 1992). Transition metals are abundant on our planet and offer attractive economic prospects for their use. From the stability aspect, alloying of transition metals is commonly used for improving corrosion resistance and tensile strength (Tomashov, 1958). To the date, there was a fair amount of effort to employ the Pt-free nanohybrid CEs with relatively high electrocatalytic activity for DSC (Duan and Tang, 2018; Li et al., 2010, 2013; Yun et al., 2014). The developed nanocomposite materials combine the advantages of graphene and metal alloy, attained a high potential in applications as CE for DSCs. However, despite the low price of the starting materials, the Pt-free CE production may be very costly. The synthesis methods are quite complex and sometimes require special environmental conditions, such as calcination, autoclave treatments, or expensive vacuum facility (Li et al., 2010, 2013; Park et al., 2017).

In spite of the substantial amount of studies on the subject, the development of a Pt-free alloy/RGO nanocomposites synthesized using cost-effective and environmentally friendly methods remains a research issue of current interest. Addressing this issue, we present here the first synthesis of CoxSn1-x/RGO (0 ≤ x ≤ 1) nanohybrids using plasma-assisted co-reduction of metal precursors and GO and their application as Pt-free CEs in DSCs. The parameters of the precursor solution before exposure to plasma were optimized to achieve the highest efficiency of DSC. As a result, the implementation of the Co0.9Sn0.1/RGO CE leads to the highest PCE, which is in line with the lowest charge transfer resistance experimentally recorded along with the highest electrocatalytic activity of the Co0.9Sn0.1/RGO CE toward the reduction of I−/I3 − redox electrolytes.

Alpha-terpineol, ethylcellulose, cobalt nitrate hexahydrate (Co(NO3)2·6H2O, 98%), tin chloride pentahydrate (SnCl4·5H2O, 98%), isopropyl alcohol (IPA, 99.5%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, USA. All other chemicals were ACS grade reagents. Graphene nanoplatelets (Grade C; 750 m2/g) were obtained from XG Sciences. FTO glass (F:SnO2) (8 Ω/□) was purchased from Pilkington, USA. TiO2 paste (18NR-AO) for a scattering layer was obtained from Dyesol. TiO2 anatase paste with nanoparticle size in a range of 15–20 nm and dye (N719) and Meltonix 1170-25PF film (Sealing Agent DuPont Surlyn®) were received from Solaronix, Switzerland.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the CoxSn1-x (0 ≤ x ≤ 1) alloys hybridized on the RGO
ce:section.ce:para: First, the GO paste was prepared from graphene nanoplatelets. Graphene nanoplatelet powder was mixed with a 1 wt% solution of ethylcellulose in terpineol by a three-roll miller (Dao et al., 2014). An area of 0.7 × 0.7 cm2 on the surface of FTO-glass was covered with a 2 µm-thick layer of GO using the doctor-blade method. The area was fixed to 0.7 × 0.7 cm2 to match it with the working electrode area. For proper attachment and evaporation of unnecessary paste components (thickening agent, solvent, etc.) the samples were then heat-treated for 30 mins at 300 °C. Prior to the GO deposition, the FTO-glass was cleaned with DI water, ethanol and acetone in an ultrasonic bath.

Next, the binary alloy nanohybrids were synthesized from the mixed Co and Sn precursor solutions. 10 mM cobalt nitrate hexahydrate and 10 mM tin chloride pentahydrate in IPA were prepared and used as the precursor solutions for Co and Sn, respectively. Then, the precursor solutions were mixed at following volume ratios of 0:1, 0.1: 0.9, 0.3: 0.7, 0.5: 0.5, 0.7: 0.3, 0.9: 0.1, 1:0 to produce different mixtures of the precursor solutions.

A new synthesis route of CoSn alloy/RGO nanohybrids is presented in Fig. 1 . The synthesis can be described as follows: 3 µl of the mixture CoSn precursor solution was drop-casted on the GO substrate. Then, the samples were moved to the oven for 15 min at 70 °C, to ensure solvent evaporation. In the next step, the metal precursors were reduced using Ar plasma under atmospheric pressure. The processing parameters were optimized for the best reduction and set as follows: forward power of 150 W, reflected power of 20 W, a gas flow rate of 5 lpm, and a substrate moving speed of 1 mm/s. The reduction process was realized within 15 min. The developed atmospheric plasma-assisted method employed in this study yields the simultaneous reduction of metal precursors and GO substrate, resulting in the synthesis of bimetallic nanohybrids. The reduction of GO to RGO during dry plasma reduction (DPR) synthesis was proven in our earlier study where it was shown that the degree of clustering of the sp2 is increased in conjunction with the sp2 bond restoration (Dao et al., 2013). The same procedure was performed on the set of solutions of all suggested as the precursor ratios. In this study, the synthesized binary alloy nanohybrids are denoted as CoxSn1-x/RGO (0 ≤ x ≤ 1). The as-made CoxSn1-x/RGO on FTO were used as counter electrodes for DSC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the working electrodes and assembly of the DSCs
ce:section.ce:para: The details of the fabrication of TiO2 photo-electrodes and DSCs assembly are described elsewhere (Dao et al., 2017). Briefly, the TiO2 pastes were screen-printed onto the FTO-glass, followed by the heat treatment at 500 °C. Note that the screen-printer printing area is 0.7 × 0.7 cm2 Then samples were immersed into the N719 dye-solution for the electrode sensibilization. Next, the counter and working electrodes are attached to each other using polymer film (Meltonix 1170-25PF). The adhesion of Meltonix on glass substrates is excellent, yielding a fully transparent sealing.
Finally, the DSC is completed by the injection of the iodine-based electrolyte into the cell through the holes drilled in the CE.

The surface morphology and structure of the binary alloy nanohybrids were studied using field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM; Hitachi SU8230, Japan) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM; JEM-2100F HR, JEOL). The current density–voltage (J-V) characteristics of the DSCs were recorded under AM 1.5G simulated illumination with an ABET Technologies Sun 3000 Solar Simulator. Calibration was realized using the silicon reference cell PECSI01 (HS Technologies).

The catalytic activity of the developed CEs was evaluated using cyclic voltammetry (CV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and Tafel measurements. CV analysis was provided on the electrochemical cells with the three-electrode configuration. The developed CEs were applied as working electrodes. Ag/Ag+ electrodes were chosen as the reference electrodes. A Pt meshes served as the CEs. As the electrolyte during the CV scan, following solution was used: a mix of 10 mmol LiI, 1 mmol I2, and 1 mmol LiClO4. All the voltammetry scans were recorded in the potential range of −300 to 600 mV at a scan rate of 20 mV s−1. The EIS and Tafel curves were collected from the dummy cells (i.e. equal electrode cells). The impedance spectroscopy was recorded on the same dummy cells under open circuit condition in a frequency range of 100 kHz to 100 mHz and a perturbation amplitude of 10 mV. The experimental data were analyzed using fitting procedures in the Z-view software (v3.2c, Scribner Associates, Inc). Tafel polarization curves were measured at a scanning rate of 5 mVs−1 in the potential range of 0.6 to −0.6 V. For calculation of the exchange current density, the obtained data were analyzed with an IviumStat device.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocurrent–voltage characteristics of DSCs: Efficiency trend with the CoxSn1-x/RGO CE
ce:section.ce:para:
DSC is a regenerative photoelectrochemical solar cell operating at the molecular level. First, the interaction of the sunlight with the dye molecules in the working electrode causes photoexcitation of the electrons from the highest occupied molecular orbit (HOMO) to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbit (LUMO). Next, ultrafast electron injection from dye into the conduction band of TiO2 occurs. From the TiO2 conduction band, electrons transfer to the transparent conducting oxide (TCO) and then to the external circuit. Upon photoexcitation, the dye molecules are transited into oxidized S+ states. The current in the electrolyte is carried by the iodide/triiodide redox couple (3I−/I− 3) or redox mediators. Dox (oxidized levels) and Dred (reduced levels) represent the Gaussian distribution of the electronic density of the states of redox couple (3I−/I− 3). The vacancies in the S+ states trap electrons released from the Dred set of levels, the redox potential of which overlaps the energy interval S+ states. The ion
I
3
-
reaches CE with diffusion, where they are reduced to iodide ions as follows: I− 3 + 2e− = 3I−.

It is well known that the efficiency of the DSC is affected by electrical conductivity and electrocatalytic activity of CE. The efficient reduction of triiodide to iodide ions (3I−/I− 3) on the surface of the CE requires low charge transfer resistance, Rct , yielding the low overpotential. In order to increase the efficiency of DSCs, the optimization of the catalyst properties of the CEs is necessary. For this purpose, the set of mixtures consisting of the Co and Sn precursor solutions with volume ratio of 0.0/1.0, 0.1/0.9, 0.3/0.7, 0.5/0.5, 0.7/0.3, 0.9/0.1, 1.0/0.0 was prepared. CoxSn1-x/RGO (0 ≤ x ≤ 1) nanocomposite materials were synthesized from the corresponding precursor mixtures. The obtained nanohybrid materials used as CEs are further referred to as Co1Sn0/RGO, Co0.9Sn0.1/RGO, Co0.7Sn0.3/RGO, Co0.5Sn0.5/RGO, Co0.3Sn0.7/RGO, Co0.1Sn0.9/RGO, and Co0Sn1/RGO.

The CEs based on CoxSn1-x/RGO (0 ≤ x ≤ 1) nanohybrids were assembled into DSCs right after plasma treatment. The photovoltaic (PV) performances of the DSCs were assessed using J-V characteristics. The J-V curves for the DSCs with representative electrodes are depicted in Fig. 2 and corresponding PV parameters are summed up in Table 1 . The Voc is defined by the difference between the quasi-Fermi level of the TiO2 under illumination and the Fermi level in the dark (Peter, 2007). Quasi-Fermi level of TiO2 strictly depends on the working electrode properties – specific surface area, absorbance, dopant concentration, etc. As for the Fermi-level under the dark, it is mostly defined by the nature of the electrolyte and working electrode of the DSC. Therefore, logically we did not observe significant changes in the voltage of the cells upon changes of the CEs. As for the Jsc, the working electrode again plays an important role. However, it is also indirectly influenced by the CE. In particular, the photo-current depends on the injection efficiency of the electrons into the TiO2. Besides, the dye photo-electronic properties depend on how fast dye is regenerated by the electrolyte anions. CE material mediates the rate of the redox reaction; the higher catalytic activity of the CE material, the higher is photocurrent (considering that all other constituents remain the same). As can be seen in Fig. 2, the alteration of the CE electronic structure is directly reflected in the Jsc value. Thus, the trend in the Jsc values suggests that CE based on Co0.9Sn0.1/RGO nanohybrid shows the highest catalytic activity among all tested electrodes. The Jsc value in the DSC based on this CE is at least 1 mA/cm2 higher than those of the other DSCs under study. Efficiency droningly decreases as the Co concentration decreases, with an abrupt bump at the point, where the Co/Sn ratio is 9:1. The fill factor (FF) generally depends on the total series resistance of the DSC. Commonly, there are four elements that contribute to series resistance: resistance associated with FTO and contacts (Rs), charge transfer resistance in the CE/electrolyte interface (Rct), resistance of TiO2/dye/electrolyte interface (R3) and diffusion resistance of the electrolyte (Zn) (Liu et al., 2010). For Co0.9Sn0.1/RGO, Rct is the lowest, so the apparent reason for the decreased FF is not related to the quality of the CE/electrolyte interface. The Jsc for this case is the highest, which may induce higher recombination in the TiO2/dye/electrolyte interface, thus causing increased R3. In this work, we exclusively focused on CE. Therefore, processes at the working electrode interfaces are out of the scope in this work. As shown in the Fig. 2, the Jsc of the Co0.3Sn0.7/RGO is lower than Co0.9Sn0.1/RGO. Thus, the recombination at the TiO2/dye/electrolyte interface is the lowest, resulting in the increase of FF. The FF values for Sn-rich alloys decrease with the increase of the Sn concentration because of the rise in the charge transfer resistance as shown in Table 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterization of the CoxSn1-x/RGO CEs
Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and Tafel measurements
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the electrochemical performance of the developed CEs, we fabricated and analyzed the symmetrical dummy cells consisting of two identical counter electrodes. EIS results are presented in Fig. 3b. Unlike FTO, RGO has a very high active surface area and therefore the Rct value of bare RGO may reach the value of ~20 Ohm/cm2 (Dao et al., 2014). As for alloyed RGO, the charge-transfer resistance value significantly varies from 1.70 to 35.23 Ohm/cm2. It appears that the presence of pure tin on the RGO is rather dampening the catalytic reaction. Tin and tin-oxide have no catalytic activity towards the iodide/tri-iodide redox reaction. However, the beneficial effect of tin on the electronic structure of cobalt upon alloying cannot be neglected. We discovered that alloying as much as 10 wt% of tin to cobalt has a beneficial effect on the catalytic activity towards redox reaction in the classic DSC. The obtained EIS data was fitted using Z-view software using a well-accepted equivalent circuit scheme for the RGO-based dummy cells (Kwon et al., 2012). The values obtained from the fitting procedure are presented in Table 1.

As can be seen in Table 1, the Rct value reaches its minimum when there is a decrease in the volume percentage of the Co(NO3)2 in the mix of precursor solutions from 100 to 90%. Further increase of the Sn concentration in the alloy significantly increases the charge transfer resistance, indicating the lowering of catalytic activity.

The analyses of the results of Bode phase plots and Tafel polarization curves are shown in Fig. 3c and d. Two similar trends such as the peak frequency in the Bode EIS plots (fmax) and the exchange current density (J0 ) values were detected. The J0 follows next trend: Co0.9Sn0.1/RGO > Co1Sn0/RGO > Co0.7Sn0.3/RGO > Co0.5Sn0.5/RGO > Co0.3Sn0.7/RGO > Co0.1Sn0.9/RGO > Co0Sn1/RGO. The fmax is the highest for the Co0.9Sn0.1/RGO and the significantly decreases with the increase of Sn concentration. Note the electron lifetime (τ) value for triiodide ion reduction is determined as 1/2πfmax, (Fig. 3c) and the J0 = RT/nFRct , where R, T, n, and F are the gas constant, absolute temperature, number of electrons involved in the reduction of iodide electrolyte, and Faraday's constant, respectively, in the Tafel measurements. The obtained results show that decreases in τ lead to increases in J0 . The shorter electron lifetime indicated accelerated electron transfer across the CE/electrolyte interface (Jeong et al., 2012), thus logically current must increase. Similar trends were detected for charge transfer resistance. The collective effect of shorter electron lifetime and lowest Rct led to the maximum short-circuit value for the Co0.9Sn0.1/RGO based DSC (Hod et al., 2011). The diffusion impedance (Zw ) values of the electrodes are also given in Table 1. As can be seen, Zw follows the same trend as Rct . Thus, the obtained results are consistent with the CV analysis and Tafel measurements.

The catalytic activity of the nanoparticles is linked to their morphological properties and facet structure. Therefore, to gain insight into the reason for the increased catalytic activity of the Co0.9Sn0.1/RGO, the SEM analysis was conducted.

Besides better catalytic performance, the Co0.9Sn-0.1/RGO is also very stable as evidenced by the 100-cycle CV measurements, shown in Fig. 4 . No noticeable changes were detected after cycling Co0.9Sn-0.1/RGO CE in the electrolyte solution. The results indicate that there was no degradation of the nanohybrid and no detachment of the film from the FTO glass. The observed results demonstrate the excellent stability of the Co0.9Sn0.1/RGO film under electrochemical reaction conditions. Good stability of the Co0.9Sn-0.1/RGO is an expected outcome, due to several reasons. Firstly, alloying of metals is a well-known method for improving the stability and corrosion resistance of metals. Secondly, carbon derivatives are proven to be stable for a variety of reactions, one of which is
I
3
-
/
I
-
(Dao et al., 2014). Furthermore, the interaction between metal nanoparticles and RGO is beneficial for stability (Dao et al., 2013).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structure of the CoSn/RGO CEs
ce:section.ce:para:
It is well known that the catalyst properties of the material are strongly influenced by size, shape and the loading of metal NPs immobilized on supporter (Yeh et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2013). To more deeply understand the relation of the catalyst properties and morphology of developed CEs, the SEM analysis was performed. The representative HR-SEM surface images of the CoxSn1-x/RGO nanohybrids (x: 1, 0.9, 0.5, 0.1, 0) are given in Fig. 5 . As can be seen, the CoxSn1-x NPs are immobilized on the surfaces of the RGO. The surface of graphene flakes is not completely covered with NPs. The underlying surface structure of graphene flakes is clearly seen in all micrographs (Fig. 5). RGO has a 3D network structure with graphene flakes in size range of 100–500 nm, providing pathways for rapid electron transfer. The high-density dispersion of NPs on the RGO surface is visible in all images except the case when monometallic Co NPs were loaded. Alloying the Co with Sn significantly increases surface coverage by the alloy particles. It is clearly seen that most of the alloy nanoparticles tend to aggregate into dendrite-like formations with significantly higher surface coverage, unlike the monometallic Co/RGO, Sn/RGO. Both aggregates and big spherical particles can be seen on the surface of RGO. Comparing to the aggregates, spherical particles exhibit lower surface area, therefore their presence is less likely to have a positive effect on catalytic activity. As we increase the Co content in the CoxSn1-x/RGO, the number and total occupied area of the spherical particles significantly reduces (highlighted by the red dash-line ellipses). It can be seen that the surface coverage of the Co0.9Sn0.1 alloy NPs was the highest among all tested samples (Fig. 5), suggesting that nanohybrid synthesized using this precursor ratio has the highest surface area among all alloy nanohybrids. The observed morphology of Co0.9Sn0.1 alloy-loaded composite supplies numerous catalytic sites and can promote electron conductivity. Both factors accelerate the reduction reaction of triiodide ions at interfaces (Peter, 2007; Dao et al., 2014; Xiao and Han, 2015). The result is consistent with the lowest Rct and highest J
o values obtained for the Co0.9Sn0.1/RGO CE.

To gain insights on the morphological structure and confirm the formation of the intermetallic alloy on the RGO, TEM and HR-TEM analyses were carried out on the Co0.9Sn0.1/RGO sample. Representative TEM images are shown in Fig. 6 . Similarly to the SEM image (Fig. 5), the NPs and particle agglomerates are presented on the surface of RGO. According to Fig. 6a, the number of particles in each aggregation may vary. The aggregates are formed by the rounded and ellipsoidal particles that have a similar gross area of 2000 nm2 (Fig. 6e and f). HR-TEM image allows studying a single particle. Synthesizing CoSn NPs, we anticipate to create a Co-Sn nanoalloy and benefit from the synergetic effect between two metals. The corresponding FFT-image is shown in Fig. 6d. Scale lattice constants were determined using two methods: graphical software from TEM micrograph given in Fig. 6c and FFT-image (Fig. 6d). In both cases, we obtained the following lattice constants: a = 4.23 Å, b = 2.58 Å. Found lattice constants are identified as Co0.9Sn0.1 nanoalloy with the hexagonally close-packed crystal structure (Buschow et al., 1983). During synthesis, we observe the mixing of the metals on the atomic level, as we confirmed the metal–metal bond.

In summary, CoxSn1-x/RGO (0 ≤ x ≤ 1) nanocomposites were synthesized using the plasma-assisted co-reduction of the oxygen functional groups of the GO and metal precursors along with the immobilization of CoSn NPs on the surface of RGO under atmospheric pressure. The optimization of the alloy structure led to improved PCE of 5.36% obtained for DSC based on Co0.9Sn-0.1/RGO. The stability of the developed nanohybrid against the long term operation was confirmed by the CV measurements. Improved efficiency was achieved due to the higher catalytic activity of the CE. Favorable precursor ratio upon mixing led to the formation of the Co0.9Sn0.1 alloy. According to SEM images, Co0.9Sn0.1 has the highest surface area supplying the highest amount of the reactive sites for the electrolyte reduction reaction. This is the reason why the Co0.9Sn0.1/RGO CE has shown the highest catalytic activity among all of the synthesized nanohybrids. Using HR-TEM analysis, the crystal structure of the Co0.9Sn0.1 was defined to be a hexagonally close-packed structure. Thus, the developed atmospheric plasma-assisted approach offers a pathway for synthesis of low-cost, stable, and earth-abundant nanohybrid CEs with high catalytic activities for a practical DSCs.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Grant (NRF-2017R1A2B2001911, NRF-2017R1A4A1015360) and Brain Pool Program (Grant No. 2019H1D3A2A01062040) through the National Research Foundation (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Science and ICT, Republic of Korea.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Boschloo and Hagfeldt, 2009
